system_prompt:
You are writing the "statement of outputs" — a human-readable narrative that
synthesizes everything the book teaches into a coherent description of the master/work's method.

The user will give you the book-level distillation, the chapter summaries, and
the structured systems-draft JSON you just derived. Write the statement.

Structure:

  # <Work Title> — Statement of Outputs

  ## Overview
  (2-3 paragraphs on what the book is and what it teaches, citing the
  book-level distillation.)

  ## Systems
  For each system in the systems-draft, one ## section. For each:
    - one paragraph describing the system in plain language
    - the system's members listed
    - the traversal rules and modification rules listed with their
      engine_payload kinds shown in code formatting
    - citations back to the relevant chapter summaries that backed each
      claim (e.g. "(Chapter 2 summary; ch02.md quotes)")

  ## Pending Work
  List every `_pending:<kebab>` engine_payload.kind value with what it
  signals. If there are none, omit this section.

  ## Provenance Notes
  Brief: what's drawn from what. Note any chapters that didn't yield a
  system and why.

This is the human-facing artifact. Be precise, cite, and don't invent
content outside the summaries and quote files.

# Book-level distillation — beginners-guide

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc and Pedagogical Structure

This book teaches jazz guitar in a deliberate sequence that moves from harmonic foundation to rhythmic application to melodic improvisation, then grounds everything in real repertoire before systematizing technique. Chapter 1 establishes the entire harmonic vocabulary as a moveable-root system—five chord qualities anchored to the ii–V–I–vi progression—while simultaneously introducing the rhythmic comping figures (Freddie Green, anticipation, Charleston) that will govern accompaniment throughout. Chapter 2 immediately applies that foundation to five canonical jazz standards, establishing the book's depth-over-breadth principle and its ear-first methodology (listen to recordings before touching the fretboard). Chapters 3 and 4 then systematize the soloing tools: arpeggios in Chapter 3 as broken-chord melodic devices independent of comping shapes, and scales in Chapter 4 as harmonically-assigned color sources deployed by the rule "when the chords change, you change." Chapter 5 extends this into a lick vocabulary that names specific bebop and blues techniques, and Chapter 6 unifies everything in the jazz blues form as a harmonic and expressive proving ground. The Roman numeral appendix (Chapter 7) supplies the notational infrastructure—capital vs. small numerals encoding chord quality—that the book's chord charts presuppose.

## The Master's Characteristic Systems and Terminology

The book operates on two interlocking systems: a moveable-root harmonic system and a function-assigned scale/arpeggio system. In the first, every chord shape is defined by a red dot at the root, so the chord's name follows its physical position on the neck—the transposition rule is "root = chord name." In the second, each scale or arpeggio is assigned to a specific harmonic function rather than to a key center, so harmonic minor belongs to the tonic when its V7 is altered, melodic minor belongs to the m7 chord as expressive tension, and so on. These two systems are complementary: the first governs comping and chord knowledge, the second governs improvisation choices.

The book's load-bearing terminology clusters around three domains. Harmonic vocabulary: maj7, dominant 7, m7, half-diminished 7, diminished 7 as the five essential qualities; ii–V–I as the master progression; tritone substitution (a 7th chord a tritone away preserving the 3rd and 7th); Bird blues (Imaj7 tonic); quick-change blues; V7alt (altered dominant); turnaround substitutions (bVI–V–I, ii–V–I, biii–bVI/ii–V–I in minor blues). Melodic/soloing vocabulary: approach notes (one fret below, resolving to chord tone); enclosures (surrounding a target note with tones one fret above and below); the 3-to-9 arpeggio (diminished 7 from the 3rd of any 7th chord); side-stepping (alternating minor pentatonic scales a half step apart); the phrygian dominant scale (fifth mode of harmonic minor, for V7 chords); blue notes (b3 and b5); the major blues scale (major pentatonic plus b3). Rhythmic vocabulary: anticipation (placing the next bar's chord on the & of 4); the Freddie Green rhythm (quarter notes with 2-and-4 accents); the Charleston pattern; syncopation discipline requiring metronome practice.

## Rules and Prescriptions the Book Establishes

Several explicit rules recur across chapters and carry the weight of prescriptions rather than suggestions:

- Root = chord name: every moveable shape is named by the red-dot root's position, making transposition automatic.
- Ear first: listen to recordings before working anything on the fretboard.
- Depth over breadth: thoroughly master each standard or technique before moving on.
- When the chords change, you change (Joe Pass's rule): scale selection is chord-specific, not key-global.
- Voice-lead by proximity: connect arpeggios by nearness of pitches rather than restarting from the root each time.
- Arpeggios are free to start anywhere: any note of the arpeggio may begin the phrase; ordering is creative, not fixed.
- Major blues scale avoids clash by lacking a 7th, making it usable over both dominant 7 and major 7 chords without collision.
- Blue notes define blues feeling: b3 and b5 are not errors or passing tones but the essential expressive content.
- Metronome for syncopation: any syncopated comping figure must be practiced with a metronome.
- Phrase-by-phrase for repertoire: learn standards section by section, not in a single pass.
- Extract concepts from licks, do not parrot them: the goal of lick study is transferable vocabulary, not imitation.

## Explicit Methods and Methodology

The book teaches three identifiable methods with stepwise structure.

The chord-melody method (Chapter 2) specifies three simultaneous elements: bass notes on lower strings, chord voicings in the middle register, and melody confined to the upper strings. This is presented as the integration of comping and soloing into a single-instrument arrangement technique.

The arpeggio improvisation method (Chapter 3) begins by detaching arpeggios from chord shapes (they are played with plectrum, muted to prevent ringing), then assigns five moveable grips corresponding to the CAGED system, then specifies three functional uses in improvisation—outlining harmonic direction, enabling smoother voice leading, and adding color through substitutions—and finally liberates the player to start on any arpeggio tone and decorate with approach notes and enclosures.

The blues solo practice method (Chapter 6) is explicitly three-staged: (1) imitation—transcribe and reproduce recorded solos; (2) composition—write out solo lines before playing them; (3) ear training—internalize melodic choices so they arise spontaneously. This sequence parallels the broader book arc from imitation (learning licks and standards) through application (assigned scales and arpeggios) to internalization (blues as expressive proving ground).

Two named masters provide exemplar moves that the book treats as teachable templates: Charlie Christian's enclosures, lower neighbor tones after leaps, and the b3–3–5 riff mixing blue notes with diatonic intervals; and Joe Pass's chord-melody rhythm technique of splitting bass from treble with selective accent, and his minor/major pentatonic mixing over jazz blues. Kenny Burrell's chord/single-note mixing in Chapter 6 is a third named approach. These are not biographical references but technique templates the student is expected to absorb and deploy.

# Chapter 1 — Jazz Guitar Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

This chapter teaches the five essential jazz chord qualities (maj7, dominant 7, minor 7, half diminished 7, diminished 7) using a moveable root-position system where the red dot indicates the chord's root and determines its name, combined with specific right-hand fingerpicking technique (thumb on bass, fingers 1-3 on upper voices) and the foundational ii–V–I–vi progression drawn from the major scale. The master's characteristic pedagogical moves appear in the transposition rule ("root = chord name") and the systematic progression from steady comping rhythms through the Freddie Green rhythm (quarter notes with 2-and-4 accents) to more sophisticated figures like anticipation and the Charleston pattern, each with explicit practice rules (metronome for syncopation, phrase-by-phrase learning for repertoire). Load-bearing terminology introduced includes red dot notation for root, chord tone numbers for quality designation, anticipation (placing next-bar's chord on the & of 4), the Freddie Green rhythm, and the Charleston—all of which establish the harmonic and rhythmic vocabulary for beginning jazz guitarists.

# Chapter 2 — Playing Jazz Standards

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

This chapter teaches five core jazz standards—Birks' Works, Summertime, Maiden Voyage, Blue Bossa, and Autumn Leaves—establishing depth-over-breadth as the method's priority and recordings as the ear-first principle that precedes fretboard work. The master's characteristic move is bridging rock/blues players into jazz vocabulary: minor pentatonic soloing over Summertime, syncopation discipline in bossa grooves, and key transposition as a transferable discipline across tunes that share harmonic families. The load-bearing terminology introduced includes turnaround substitutions (bVI-V-I, ii-V-I, biii-bVI/ii-V-I in minor blues), modal harmony and Dorian modes, ii-V-I progressions in both major and minor forms, syncopation in comping and soloing, and chord melody as the three-element technique of playing bass, chords, and melody simultaneously with melody confined to the upper strings.

# Chapter 3 — Jazz Guitar Arpeggios

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches the foundational technique of jazz guitar arpeggios—broken chords played with plectrum and muted to prevent ringing, independent from chord shapes and used for soloing rather than accompaniment—by anchoring all arpeggio shapes to the ii-V-I progression and introducing five movable grips for major, minor, and dominant chords that correspond to the CAGED system. The chapter establishes three functional purposes for arpeggios in improvisation: outlining harmonic direction, enabling smoother voice leading, and adding color through substitutions. Master technique surfaces in the voice-leading rule (connecting arpeggios by proximity rather than restarting from roots) and the creative liberation principle that arpeggios can start on any note and be reordered at will. Key terminology introduced includes arpeggio patterns (5ths/4ths skipping, groups of three), movable shapes, approach notes (chromatic tension created by approaching from one fret below, resolving to chord tones), and enclosures (the bebop decoration of surrounding each chord tone with notes one fret above and below).

# Chapter 4 — Jazz Guitar Scales

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 teaches the deployment of four scales—major blues, natural minor (Aeolian), harmonic minor, and melodic minor—each assigned to specific chords within the minor ii–V–I progression and other jazz contexts, with the governing principle that scale choice follows harmonic change (Joe Pass's "When the chords change, you change"). The master's characteristic move is to justify each scale's use through a single differentiating feature: the major blues scale's b3 as the "blue note," harmonic minor's raised 7th that generates V7(b9,b13) tensions over the altered dominant, and melodic minor's raised 7th as an expressive tension device over m7 chords. The load-bearing terminology introduced includes the major blues scale's avoidance-of-clash rule (its lack of a 7th allows use over both dominant and major seventh chords), the Aeolian mode as the identity of natural minor, the V7alt (altered dominant) as the harmonic context requiring tonic harmonic minor rather than the chord's root, and the deliberate-choice model for expressive scale deployment (treating raised intervals as context-dependent decisions rather than automatic inclusions).

# Chapter 5 — Jazz Guitar Licks

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Jazz Guitar Licks teaches the core pedagogical principle of extracting transferable concepts from learned licks rather than parroting them wholesale, anchored in key terminology including blue notes (b3 and b5 from the blues scale), the phrygian dominant scale (fifth mode of harmonic minor) for V7 chords, the 3 to 9 arpeggio (dim7 from the 3rd of any 7th chord), and tritone substitution mechanics (playing a 7th chord a tritone away while preserving the 3rd and 7th). The chapter establishes essential bebop techniques such as triads for chord outlining, a balance between diatonic and chromatic lines, the enclosure technique (surrounding a target note for tension and release), and side-stepping (alternating between minor pentatonic scales a half step apart for modern jazz texture). Charlie Christian emerges as the master whose characteristic moves—enclosures, lower neighbor tones after leaps, and the b3 to 3 to 5 riff mixing blue notes with diatonic intervals—recur throughout, while additional vocabulary includes the major blues scale (major pentatonic plus b3), diminished scale construction (alternating whole and half steps), double stops over pedal tones, three-note-per-string legato adapted from rock, and Joe Pass's signature chord-melody rhythm technique of splitting bass from treble with selective accent.

# Chapter 6 — Jazz Blues

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

This chapter teaches the harmonic and melodic foundations of jazz blues by defining the characteristic chord qualities (dominant 7 tonic and basic I7-IV7-V7 changes), historically significant substitutions (Count Basie's #IVdim7 and VI7b9, bebop ii-V chains, tritone substitutions), and distinctive voice-leading techniques (contrary motion, sus chords for rhythmic tension). The load-bearing terminology includes quick change blues progression, Bird blues (defined by its Imaj7 tonic), tritone substitution, and the scale-mixing strategies that produce the jazz-blues sound: b3 and 3 mixing (minor blues + Mixolydian), Lydian Dominant over tritone substitutes, and harmonic minor over dominant 7b9 chords. The chapter highlights two master approaches—Kenny Burrell's chord/single-note mixing technique and Joe Pass's minor/major pentatonic mixing—while emphasizing that blues is defined not only by harmonic structure but by blue notes and sound/feeling, anchored in three structured solo practice methods that progress from imitation through composition to ear training.

# Chapter 7 — Appendix - The Roman Numeral System

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

This appendix introduces the Roman numeral system for labeling chord function in jazz, establishing capital Roman numerals (I, V, V7) for major and dominant chords and small Roman numerals (ii, iii, vi, vii°) for minor and diminished/half-diminished chords—a notational convention that distinguishes chord labeling from Arabic scale-degree numbering. The system extends beyond diatonic harmony to accommodate chromatic alterations and substitutions like tritone substitutions, marked with flats and sharps when chords fall outside the basic key center. The key load-bearing terminology introduced are Roman numeral chord labels themselves, along with the distinction between capital and small numeral notation as an encoding of chord quality that readers must internalize to interpret chord charts throughout the book. This is a reference appendix rather than an instructional chapter.
